preFix.title: Courgette and lemon loaf
preFix.subtitle: A summer-glut quick bread, fragrant and very moist
preFix.excerpt: Courgette and lemon loaf uses grated courgette to keep the crumb unusually moist, with lemon zest cutting through any heaviness. A batter loaf, no kneading required. The courgette all but disappears into the crumb, leaving a clean, fresh flavour. A good recipe when the allotment is producing more courgettes than the kitchen can use.
preFix.sourceNotes: Vegetable quick breads incorporating courgette or marrow appear widely in British and American domestic baking from the mid-twentieth century onward, particularly as summer-glut recipes when vegetable-garden production outstrips household demand. The lemon pairing is a modern British development, providing sharpness to balance the neutral flavour of the courgette. This recipe follows the standard all-in-one batter method.

content.text: Courgette contains a great deal of water, which it releases gradually as the loaf bakes. This is what makes the crumb so moist. There is no need to squeeze the grated courgette dry before adding it; the moisture is part of the recipe. Grate it on the coarse side of a box grater and use it immediately, before it weeps too much water onto the board.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin and line with baking paper. In a large bowl, combine 225 g plain flour, 2 tsp baking powder, half a teaspoon each of bicarbonate of soda and salt, and 150 g caster sugar. Stir in the zest of 2 lemons. In a separate bowl, beat 2 large eggs with 100 ml vegetable oil and the juice of half a lemon. Stir in 250 g coarsely grated courgette. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and fold with a spatula until just combined. Pour into the prepared tin. Bake for 50 to 60 minutes until a skewer inserted through the centre comes out clean and the top is golden. Leave in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.

While the loaf is still warm, whisk 80 g icing sugar with the remaining lemon juice to a thick, pourable consistency. Spoon over the top and leave to set for 15 minutes. The glaze soaks into the crumb and sharpens the lemon flavour.

items.problem: Loaf is wet in the middle after baking
items.solution: Different courgettes release different amounts of water. If the loaf is consistently wet after the stated time, grate the courgette and leave it in a sieve for 10 minutes before using, then squeeze out the excess water.

items.problem: Loaf is very pale on top
items.solution: Fan ovens vary. If the top is pale at 50 minutes but the skewer is clean, raise the temperature to 180 degrees C and bake for a further 5 minutes to colour the crust.
